subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Duty of Candour to the Tribunal — Rule 5.1-2
fact_pattern: Lawyer Rosa is representing a defendant in a civil action. During her opening statement, she tells the judge that a particular witness is available to testify and will provide key evidence. Before the witness testifies, Rosa learns that the witness has left the country and will not be returning in time for trial. Rosa decides not to inform the court because she fears it will weaken her client's position and hopes the matter resolves before the issue arises.
call_of_question: Has Rosa breached her duty of candour to the tribunal under Rule 5.1-2?

options.A: No — Rosa's opening statement was made in good faith at the time and she has no obligation to correct information that has since become inaccurate.
options.B: No — the duty of candour applies only to written submissions, not to oral statements made during trial.
options.C: Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making false statements to the tribunal and requires the lawyer to correct a false statement previously made; Rosa must inform the court that the witness will not be available.
options.D: No — strategic decision-making about what information to share with the court is within the scope of the advocate's discretion.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct imposes a duty of candour on lawyers appearing before tribunals. This duty includes not knowingly making false or misleading statements to the tribunal and, critically, correcting any false statement previously made to the court. Rosa initially represented to the judge that the witness would be available. Having learned this is no longer true, she is obligated to correct that representation. Allowing the court to proceed on a false basis violates her duty of candour and constitutes a breach of Rule 5.1-2.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The duty of candour includes a positive obligation to correct false statements previously made to the tribunal. The fact that the statement was made in good faith at the time does not eliminate the duty to correct once the lawyer becomes aware of the falsehood.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. The duty of candour applies to all communications with the tribunal, including oral statements made during trial. It is not limited to written submissions.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making false statements to the tribunal and requires the lawyer to correct a false statement previously made; Rosa must inform the court that the witness will not be available.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty of candour is not subject to override by strategic advocacy considerations. A lawyer's obligations to the court take precedence over tactical client interests when a false statement has been made.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty of candour — correcting false statement to court

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — duty of candour to tribunal
obligation to correct false statement previously made
duty of candour applies to oral and written submissions

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Misleading the Court by Omission — Citing Contrary Authorities
fact_pattern: Lawyer Stephan is researching a motion for summary judgment. He finds a recent Ontario Court of Appeal decision that directly contradicts the position he is advancing for his client. He also finds an older Superior Court decision that supports his client's position. Stephan decides to cite only the Superior Court decision in his factum and omit any reference to the Court of Appeal decision, hoping opposing counsel will not find it.
call_of_question: Does Stephan's failure to cite the contrary Court of Appeal decision in his factum violate the Rules of Professional Conduct?

options.A: No — a lawyer is under no obligation to assist the opposing party by citing authorities that harm the client's position; adversarial practice permits selective citation.
options.B: No — the duty to cite contrary authorities only arises if the opposing party has specifically requested disclosure of adverse case law.
options.C: Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to cite any directly relevant authorities of which the lawyer is aware even if they are unfavourable to the lawyer's position; failure to disclose a directly contrary Court of Appeal decision misleads the court by omission.
options.D: No — the duty to cite contrary authority applies only in courts of final resort, not in motions before the Superior Court.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer, as advocate, must not deliberately fail to advise the tribunal of binding authorities that are directly relevant to the matter, even if they are unfavourable to the client's position. A recent Ontario Court of Appeal decision that directly contradicts the argument being made is exactly the type of authority that must be disclosed. Deliberately omitting it to mislead the court constitutes a breach of the duty of candour.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The adversarial system does not permit selective citation designed to mislead the court. Rule 5.1-2 imposes an obligation to cite directly contrary binding authorities regardless of their impact on the client's position.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to disclose contrary authorities is not contingent on a request from opposing counsel. It is a positive and proactive obligation under Rule 5.1-2.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to cite any directly relevant authorities of which the lawyer is aware even if they are unfavourable to the lawyer's position; failure to disclose a directly contrary Court of Appeal decision misleads the court by omission.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to disclose contrary binding authorities applies in all courts and tribunals before which a lawyer appears, not only in courts of final resort.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty of candour — failure to cite contrary authority

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — duty to cite directly contrary binding authorities
misleading the court by omission
candour applies even when authority hurts client

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Duty Not to Bring Frivolous Proceedings — Rule 5.1-2(e)
fact_pattern: Lawyer Teresa is consulted by a client who wants to sue a neighbour for $500 for an alleged breach of a verbal agreement. The client acknowledges that the verbal agreement is not provable and that no loss was actually suffered — the client simply wants to use the lawsuit to harass the neighbour following a personal dispute. Teresa considers filing the claim.
call_of_question: What are Teresa's obligations under Rule 5.1-2 and the Rules of Professional Conduct regarding the proposed action?

options.A: Teresa must file the claim because a lawyer is obligated to advance whatever position the client instructs.
options.B: Teresa must file the claim because the client has a right of access to courts and the merits of the claim are for a judge to assess.
options.C: Teresa must not commence proceedings that have no merit and are brought for an improper purpose; Rule 5.1-2(e) prohibits a lawyer from bringing proceedings that are without a reasonable basis and intended to abuse the court process.
options.D: Teresa may file the claim as long as she informs the client that the claim is unlikely to succeed.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2(e) of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct provides that a lawyer must not knowingly assist or permit a client to do anything that the lawyer considers to be dishonest or dishonourable, and more specifically, must not institute proceedings that have no merit and are brought for an improper purpose. A lawsuit filed to harass rather than to vindicate a legitimate legal right is frivolous and an abuse of process. Teresa must decline to commence this action.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A lawyer is not obligated to advance any position a client instructs. The duty to not bring frivolous or harassing proceedings is a constraint on the lawyer's role as advocate.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. The right of access to courts does not include the right to use courts as instruments of harassment. Rule 5.1-2 limits what a lawyer may do even if a client demands it.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Teresa must not commence proceedings that have no merit and are brought for an improper purpose; Rule 5.1-2(e) prohibits a lawyer from bringing proceedings that are without a reasonable basis and intended to abuse the court process.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Informing the client of the low likelihood of success does not cure the problem. The proceeding is improper regardless of whether the client is warned, and Teresa must decline to file it.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: no frivolous proceedings — abuse of process

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2(e) — no frivolous or harassing proceedings
lawyer cannot file claims for improper purpose
client instruction does not override duty to court

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Duty Not to Assert Position Without Foundation — Rule 5.1-2
fact_pattern: Lawyer Ulrich is defending a commercial landlord in a wrongful eviction action. The tenant has strong evidence of the eviction. Ulrich's client instructs him to raise every possible defence, including defences that Ulrich knows have absolutely no legal or factual foundation, simply to delay the proceedings and impose maximum costs on the tenant. Ulrich proceeds as instructed.
call_of_question: Has Ulrich acted in accordance with his duties under the Rules of Professional Conduct?

options.A: Yes — a defence lawyer's duty is to zealously advocate for the client by raising all possible defences; Ulrich is obligated to follow his client's instructions.
options.B: Yes — the court can assess costs against the landlord at the end of the proceedings, which is the appropriate mechanism to address unfounded defences.
options.C: No — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from asserting a position in proceedings where the lawyer knows the position is not supported by law and has no merit; using unfounded defences to delay proceedings and harass the opposing party is a breach of the advocate's duty.
options.D: Yes — as long as the unfounded defences are technically available at law, raising them is a legitimate tactical decision within the advocate's discretion.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct limits the advocate's role by prohibiting the assertion of positions in proceedings that the lawyer knows to be without merit. A lawyer cannot use litigation as a tool of delay and harassment by pleading defences that have no legal or factual foundation. Zealous advocacy does not extend to advancing knowingly baseless positions. The availability of costs as a remedy at the end of proceedings does not excuse the lawyer from complying with Rule 5.1-2 during the proceedings.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Zealous advocacy does not include advancing positions the lawyer knows to have no merit. Rule 5.1-2 restricts the advocate's role regardless of client instructions.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. The availability of a costs remedy does not authorize conduct that violates Rule 5.1-2 during the proceedings. The obligation is a constraint on the lawyer's conduct, not a matter to be remedied at the end.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. No — Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from asserting a position in proceedings where the lawyer knows the position is not supported by law and has no merit; using unfounded defences to delay proceedings and harass the opposing party is a breach of the advocate's duty.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Technical legal availability does not make a defence appropriate to raise when the lawyer knows it has no merit. Rule 5.1-2 requires that positions advanced must have a reasonable legal and factual basis.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: unmeritorious defence — asserting position without foundation

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — no positions without legal/factual foundation
zealous advocacy has limits
lawyer cannot follow client instruction to raise baseless defences

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Knowingly Adducing Perjured Evidence — Mandatory Withdrawal
fact_pattern: Lawyer Valentina is defending an accused charged with assault. During trial preparation, the accused tells Valentina that the alibi witness Valentina intends to call will be lying — the accused was actually at the scene but claims his alibi witness has agreed to provide false testimony. The accused insists Valentina proceed and call the alibi witness anyway, since the Crown cannot disprove the alibi.
call_of_question: What must Valentina do under Rule 5.1-2 of the Rules of Professional Conduct?

options.A: Valentina must call the alibi witness because the accused has the right to present a full defence and the Crown's burden to disprove the alibi is not affected by what Valentina knows privately.
options.B: Valentina must not call a witness she knows will give false testimony; she must advise the accused that she cannot present knowingly false evidence and that she must withdraw if the accused persists in the instruction; Valentina may not knowingly adduce perjured evidence regardless of the accused's instructions.
options.C: Valentina may call the witness because solicitor-client privilege protects her from having to act on information received from the accused during a privileged consultation.
options.D: Valentina must immediately disclose to the Crown that the alibi is false because her duty to the court overrides solicitor-client privilege in criminal proceedings.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from knowingly adducing false or perjured evidence before a tribunal. Once Valentina knows the intended alibi evidence is false, she cannot present it regardless of the accused's instructions. She must advise the accused that she cannot proceed on those instructions and that she will withdraw if he persists. Rule 5.1-2(f) makes clear that a lawyer must not, with knowledge that evidence is false, offer it. Valentina may not disclose the contents of the privileged communication to the Crown — but she must refuse to act on the instruction.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The right to make full answer and defence does not extend to presenting evidence the lawyer knows to be false. The duty to the court under Rule 5.1-2 prohibits knowingly adducing perjured evidence.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Valentina must not call a witness she knows will give false testimony; she must advise the accused that she cannot present knowingly false evidence and that she must withdraw if the accused persists in the instruction; Valentina may not knowingly adduce perjured evidence regardless of the accused's instructions.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Solicitor-client privilege protects Valentina from disclosing the communication, but it does not authorize her to act on a privileged instruction to present false evidence. Privilege protects the communication from disclosure; it does not authorize misconduct.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Valentina must not disclose to the Crown — the privileged communication remains protected. Her response is to refuse the instruction and withdraw if necessary, not to breach privilege.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2(f) (knowing adduction of perjured evidence)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty to tribunal — knowing adduction of perjured evidence; mandatory withdrawal

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2(f) — cannot knowingly adduce perjured evidence
client instruction does not authorize presenting false evidence
privilege protects communication but does not authorize misconduct

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Duty Not to Mislead Court — Silence and Omission
fact_pattern: Lawyer Winston is acting for a plaintiff in a motion for summary judgment. During oral argument, the judge asks Winston directly whether there is any evidence contradicting the plaintiff's version of events. Winston is aware of an email that seriously undermines the plaintiff's credibility on the key issue but that has not been produced by either party. Winston says 'no' to the judge's question.
call_of_question: Has Winston's answer to the judge's question breached his duty of candour under Rule 5.1-2?

options.A: No — Winston is not required to disclose evidence harmful to his client's case; the adversarial system places that obligation on the opposing party.
options.B: Yes — when directly asked by the court whether there is contradictory evidence, a lawyer who is aware of such evidence cannot answer falsely; Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making a false statement to the tribunal, including by giving a false answer to a direct judicial inquiry.
options.C: No — Winston's obligation of confidentiality to his client prevents him from disclosing the email or acknowledging that it exists.
options.D: No — because the email has not been produced, it is not properly before the court and Winston has no duty to acknowledge its existence.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making a false statement to a tribunal. When a judge directly asks whether contradictory evidence exists, a lawyer who is aware of such evidence cannot answer 'no' — that is a knowing false statement to the tribunal. The adversarial system does not authorize lying to a judge in response to a direct question. Winston was not required to volunteer the email unprompted, but once directly asked, he could not falsely deny knowledge of contradictory evidence.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. While lawyers are generally not required to volunteer information harmful to their client, they cannot answer a direct judicial question falsely. Winston was directly asked and gave a false answer.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — when directly asked by the court whether there is contradictory evidence, a lawyer who is aware of such evidence cannot answer falsely; Rule 5.1-2 prohibits a lawyer from knowingly making a false statement to the tribunal, including by giving a false answer to a direct judicial inquiry.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The duty of confidentiality does not authorize making false statements to the court. Winston could have declined to answer or said he could not answer without breaching confidence — but he could not say 'no' falsely.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. The existence of the email in Winston's knowledge is the relevant fact, not whether it has been formally produced. A lawyer cannot deny knowing about evidence simply because it has not been formally produced.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (duty not to mislead — false answer to direct judicial inquiry)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty of candour — false answer to direct judicial inquiry

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — cannot knowingly make false statement to tribunal
false answer to direct judicial inquiry is breach of candour
confidentiality does not authorize lying to court

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Ex Parte Communications with the Court
fact_pattern: Lawyer Xavier is representing a plaintiff in a civil action. Frustrated by what he sees as excessive delays in scheduling a motion, he telephones the chambers of the presiding judge directly without notice to opposing counsel to request an urgent hearing date and to explain his concerns about the scheduling process. The judge's assistant takes a message. When opposing counsel later learns of the call, she objects.
call_of_question: Has Xavier engaged in improper ex parte communication with the court, and is the objection well-founded?

options.A: No — contacting the court's scheduling office or chambers to request a hearing date is an administrative matter that is not subject to the prohibition on ex parte communications.
options.B: Yes — any direct communication with the court, including to scheduling staff or chambers, without notice to opposing counsel regarding the substance of the proceeding is improper ex parte communication; Xavier should have communicated through proper channels, such as a consent motion or scheduling conference notice to all parties.
options.C: No — ex parte communications with the court are only prohibited when they are made in writing; an oral telephone message does not violate any rule.
options.D: No — because Xavier did not actually speak with the judge, no ex parte communication occurred.

explanation: The prohibition on ex parte communications with the court applies to any substantive communication with the court or judicial staff about a proceeding, without the knowledge of opposing counsel. Contacting chambers to complain about scheduling and request a hearing is not a purely administrative scheduling request — it involves conveying the lawyer's view of the scheduling process to judicial staff, which is a substantive communication about the matter. All parties are entitled to be present when any such communication is made. Xavier should have written to all parties and used the court's formal scheduling procedures to address his concerns.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. While routine scheduling communications may be handled directly, contacting chambers to convey concerns about scheduling delays and request priority consideration crosses from purely administrative to substantive communication that all parties should receive notice of.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — any direct communication with the court, including to scheduling staff or chambers, without notice to opposing counsel regarding the substance of the proceeding is improper ex parte communication; Xavier should have communicated through proper channels, such as a consent motion or scheduling conference notice to all parties.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The medium of communication — oral vs. written — is irrelevant to whether the communication is improper. The prohibition applies to substantive ex parte communications regardless of form.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. An ex parte communication does not require the lawyer to speak with the judge personally. Communicating substantively with chambers staff about a proceeding without opposing counsel's knowledge is equally problematic.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (ex parte communications with court)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty to tribunal — improper ex parte communication

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — prohibition on ex parte communications with court
substantive communication with chambers requires notice to all parties
form of communication irrelevant to ex parte prohibition

subject: professional_responsibility
chapter_title: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
subtopic_heading: Disclosure of Adverse Legal Authority to the Court
fact_pattern: Lawyer Yasmine is representing a landlord in an application before the Landlord and Tenant Board. She is aware of a binding provincial court decision that directly contradicts the statutory interpretation she is advancing, which was decided just three weeks earlier. Opposing counsel — a self-represented tenant — has not cited this decision and appears unaware of it. Yasmine decides not to mention it.
call_of_question: Does Yasmine have an obligation to disclose the contrary binding decision to the Landlord and Tenant Board even though opposing counsel has not raised it?

options.A: No — in an adversarial system, a lawyer has no obligation to cite authorities that harm the client's case; discovering and citing helpful authorities is the opposing party's responsibility.
options.B: Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to disclose to the tribunal any binding legal authority directly relevant to the matter that is adverse to the client's position, regardless of whether the opposing party has found it; the self-represented status of the tenant does not change this obligation.
options.C: No — the duty to disclose adverse legal authorities only applies in courts, not before administrative tribunals like the Landlord and Tenant Board.
options.D: No — the decision was issued only three weeks ago and is too recent to be considered binding; Yasmine has no obligation to cite newly released decisions.

explanation: Rule 5.1-2 of the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct requires a lawyer as advocate to cite relevant binding legal authorities known to the lawyer, even if unfavourable to the client's position. This duty applies before all tribunals, including administrative bodies like the Landlord and Tenant Board. The fact that the opposing party is self-represented and has not found the authority does not relieve Yasmine of the obligation — the duty runs to the tribunal, not to the opposing party. A recently issued decision is binding if it meets the applicable binding authority standard.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. The adversarial system does not permit a lawyer to withhold binding adverse authorities from the tribunal. Rule 5.1-2 imposes a positive obligation to disclose such authorities.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Yes — Rule 5.1-2 requires a lawyer as advocate to disclose to the tribunal any binding legal authority directly relevant to the matter that is adverse to the client's position, regardless of whether the opposing party has found it; the self-represented status of the tenant does not change this obligation.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The duty to disclose adverse binding authorities applies in all courts and tribunals before which a lawyer appears, including administrative tribunals such as the Landlord and Tenant Board.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. A recently issued decision is binding if it comes from a court of appropriate authority. The age of the decision does not determine whether it must be disclosed — its binding nature and relevance do.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Duties to Tribunals and Third Parties
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 5.1-2 (disclosure of adverse binding authority)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: duty to tribunal — disclosure of adverse binding authority

tested_concepts: Rule 5.1-2 — duty to disclose adverse binding authorities to tribunal
duty applies before administrative tribunals not only courts
self-represented opposing party does not change disclosure obligation
